•	Head/Neck: Mucous membranes moist. JVP not elevated. No carotid bruits. Thyroid non-enlarged.
•	Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
•	Heart: Tachycardic, irregularly irregular rhythm. Variable S1 intensity. No murmurs or gallops.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds present.
•	Extremities: Warm, well-perfused. Trace ankle edema bilaterally (chronic). No calf tenderness or asymmetry. Peripheral pulses palpable but irregular.
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
•	Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (RVR). Ventricular rate ~128 bpm. No ST-segment elevation or depression. QTc within normal limits.
Laboratory Results:
Hematology - Complete Blood Count (CBC):
•	WBC: 7.2 (10^9/L)
•	RBC: 4.10 (10^12/L)
•	Hb: 128 (g/L)
•	Hct: 0.385 (L/L)
•	Plt: 210 (10^9/L)
Biochemistry:
•	Glucose: 8.2 mmol/L
•	Urea: 5.8 mmol/L
•	Creatinine: 75 µmol/L (eGFR 72 mL/min/1.73m²)
•	Troponin T (hs): 9 ng/L (Negative, <14)
•	TSH: 2.1 mIU/L
•	AST 22, ALT 19
Electrolytes:
•	Na: 139 mmol/L
•	K: 4.1 mmol/L
•	Mg: 0.85 mmol/L
Imaging:
•	Chest X-ray (PA view): Cardiac silhouette borderline enlarged. No pulmonary edema, consolidations, or pleural effusions.
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, Radiologist, 16.07.2024, 09:30)
________________________________________
Therapy administered in ED
•	Bisoprolol 2.5 mg orally given at 09:15.
•	Infusion of 500ml 0.9% NaCl.
Observation:
•	Heart rate decreased to 95-105 bpm resting after 2 hours. Patient reports symptomatic improvement.
________________________________________
Conclusion
Patient presented with new-onset Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response. Rate control achieved with beta-blockers. Hemodynamically stable. Troponin negative, excluding acute myocardial infarction. Thyroid function normal.

Risk Stratification:
•	CHA2DS2-VASc score calculated as 1 (point for female gender).
•	HAS-BLED score: 1 (Hypertension).

Plan:
•	Discharge home.
•	Continue rate control: Bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily added to current medication list.
•	No indication for oral anticoagulation at this time based on risk score.
•	Follow up with Cardiology in 2 weeks for potential rhythm control consideration.
•	Return to ED if palpitations worsen, chest pain occurs, or syncope.

Reason for Visit
Chest pain
Anamnesis
Personal History:
Hypertension (treated with Ramipril), Hyperlipidemia. Smoker (10 pack-years).
Current Illness:
Patient presents with complaints of central chest pain that started approximately 3 hours ago while lifting boxes in the garage. Describes pain as "sharp" and non-radiating. Rates pain 6/10. Reports that the pain feels worse when twisting the torso or taking a deep breath. Denies nausea, vomiting, or diaphoresis. Mild shortness of breath noted upon exertion.
Functions:
Appetite normal. Bowel movements regular. Urination normal, no dysuria.
Chronic Therapy:
Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (Rash).
Status
Conscious, oriented x3. Well-nourished male, appears slightly uncomfortable but in no acute distress.
Head/Neck: Mucous membranes moist. JVP not elevated. No carotid bruits.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or rales. Good air entry.
Heart: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 normal. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs.
Chest Wall: Reproducible tenderness noted on palpation of the left sternal border and pectoralis muscle.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds present.
Extremities: No pedal edema. Calves non-tender. Pulses 2+ and symmetric.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
Normal Sinus Rhythm, rate 84 bpm. No ST-segment elevation or depression. T-wave inversion in lead III (non-specific). No Q waves.

Radiographic Imaging Chest PA:
Lung fields clear. No pneumothorax or pleural effusion. Cardiac silhouette within normal limits. Mediastinum not widened. No acute osseous abnormalities.

Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.85 [1e12]/L, HGB 152 g/L, Hct 0.445 L/L, MCV 91.0 fL, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L
NEU% 72.0 %, LYM% 18.5 %, MONO% 7.0 %, EOS% 2.0 %
Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L, Urea 6.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 98 µmol/L, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m²
Glucose 6.8 mmol/L
AST 22 U/L, ALT 25 U/L, LDH 180 U/L
CRP 8.5 mg/L
**hs-TNT 58.4 ng/L**
D-dimer 0.22 mg/L (Negative)

Therapy
Ketonal (Ketoprofen) 100mg IV in 100ml Saline.
Diazepam 2mg PO.

Conclusion
Patient presents with atypical chest pain. Physical exam reveals reproducible chest wall tenderness consistent with musculoskeletal strain. ECG shows no evidence of ischemia. Chest X-ray is normal. D-dimer negative, ruling out pulmonary embolism.
Condition improved after analgesic administration.
Discharge diagnosis: Musculoskeletal chest pain.
Plan: Discharge home. Continue NSAIDs (Ibuprofen 400mg) prn for pain. Follow up with GP in 3-5 days. Return to ED if pain worsens or becomes constant.
gold_reference: The report documents a High-Sensitivity Troponin T (hs-TNT) level of 58.4 ng/L. Depending on the specific assay, the upper limit of normal is typically around 14 ng/L. A value of 58.4 is significantly elevated and indicates myocardial injury (Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction or NSTEMI). The physician incorrectly attributed the pain solely to musculoskeletal causes (due to reproducible tenderness) and discharged the patient, ignoring the positive cardiac biomarker which necessitates immediate admission.

Date of Birth: 1972

Triage Category: 3
________________________________________
Diagnoses
•	R07.1: Chest pain on breathing
•	M79.1: Myalgia
________________________________________
Triage Data
•	Triage Category: 3
•	BP: 138/85 mmHg
•	Pulse: 108/min
•	SpO2: 96%
•	Temp: 37.1 °C
•	VAS: 6
•	GCS: 15
________________________________________
Vital Signs
Date	BP (mmHg)	Pulse (/min)	SpO2 (%)	Resp (/min)	Temp (°C)	Glu (mmol/L)	VAS	GCS	AVPU
22.08.2024, 14:15	140/88	110	96	20	37.2	5.6	6	15	A
________________________________________
Reason for Visit
Sharp pain in the right side of the chest, worsening with deep inspiration.
________________________________________
Medical History (Anamnesis)
Current Complaints: 
The patient reports a sudden onset of sharp, "stabbing" pain in the right hemithorax that started yesterday evening. The pain is strictly localized and worsens significantly during inhalation and coughing. Denies productive cough or hemoptysis. Reports mild shortness of breath when walking.
Medical History:
•	Post-operative status: Arthroscopic ACL reconstruction of the right knee 12 days ago. Currently mobilizing with crutches.
•	History of localized superficial thrombophlebitis in the left leg 3 years ago.
•	No known chronic respiratory or cardiac diseases.
•	Non-smoker.
Medications:
•	Andol 100 mg (discontinued 2 days ago).
•	Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for post-op pain.
Drug Allergies:
•	Penicillin (skin rash).
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General: Alert, oriented, appears in moderate discomfort during deep inspiration. Slightly tachypneic.
•	Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. Right-sided pleuritic rub not definitively heard.
•	Heart: Tachycardic, rhythmic, clear tones, no murmurs.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.
•	Extremities: Right lower extremity in a hinged knee brace. Right calf is slightly more edematous than the left (+1.5 cm circumference at mid-calf), with mild tenderness on deep palpation of the posterior compartment. No redness or increased warmth.
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
12-Lead ECG:
•	Sinus tachycardia, heart rate 109 bpm. Normal axis. No ST-segment or T-wave changes. No S1Q3T3 pattern observed.
Laboratory Results:
Hematology:
•	WBC: 9.4 (10^9/L)
•	Hb: 142 (g/L)
•	Platelets: 285 (10^9/L)
Coagulation:
•	PV: 0.98
•	INR: 1.05
•	D-dimer: 3.45 mg/L FEU (Reference range: < 0.50) [Elevated]
Biochemistry:
•	Urea: 5.2 mmol/L
•	Creatinine: 82 µmol/L
•	CRP: 12.4 mg/L (Reference: < 5.0) [Elevated]
•	Troponin T (hs-TnT): 9 ng/L (Reference: < 14)
Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (AP view):
•	Findings: Lung parenchyma shows no signs of consolidation, pneumothorax, or pleural effusion. Heart shadow is normal in size and shape. Diaphragmatic domes are sharp.
(Performed by Dr. Goran Babić, 22.08.2024, 15:45)
________________________________________
Therapy
•	Ketoprofen 100 mg IM (administered in ED)
•	0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV infusion
________________________________________
Conclusion
The patient’s chest pain is likely musculoskeletal/pleuritic in nature, possibly related to altered gait and use of crutches following knee surgery. Clinical examination of the lungs and chest X-ray are unremarkable. Laboratory findings show a non-specific elevation of D-dimer and CRP, which can be expected in the early post-operative period.

Recommendations:
•	Continue with Ibuprofen 400 mg 3x1 for pain management.
•	Follow-up with the orthopedic surgeon as scheduled.
•	Local application of warm compresses to the chest wall.
•	Return to the ED if severe dyspnea, syncope, or hemoptysis occurs.

Discharged in stable condition with improvement in pain after analgesia.
(Dr. Marina Zovko, 22.08.2024, 17:30)
gold_reference: The physician failed to order a CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA) or V/Q scan despite the patient having a high clinical probability for Pulmonary Embolism (PE). The patient has a Wells Score of 6 (Recent surgery +3, Tachycardia +1.5, Calf swelling/DVT suspicion +1.5) and a significantly elevated D-dimer (3.45 mg/L). Discharging the patient with only NSAIDs without ruling out PE is a critical failure that could lead to fatal outcomes.

report_content:
Date of birth: 1950

Triage category: 3
Diagnoses
I48.0 Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
E11 Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications
Triage Data
Triage category: 3; BP: 154/95 mmHg; Pulse: 122/min; SpO2: 95%; GCS: 15; Temp: 36.7°C
Reason for visit
Palpitations and shortness of breath
History
From the history: The patient is a 74-year-old female with a known history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. She reports a sudden onset of "heart racing" and "fluttering" in her chest that began approximately 4 hours ago while she was watching television. She describes associated mild dyspnea on exertion but denies syncope, focal neurological deficits, or severe chest pain.
Family history: Mother had a stroke at age 80.
Current illness: Symptoms have been persistent since onset. She has not experienced similar episodes in the past. 
Habits: Non-smoker, does not consume alcohol.
Functions: Appetite is preserved, no urinary or bowel complaints.
Medications: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Metformin 850 mg 2x1.
Drug allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status
Of medium build, alert and oriented x3, eupneic at rest, afebrile, skin and mucous membranes normally colored, no peripheral edema. Neck veins not distended. 
Chest: Symmetrical, normal excursions.
Lungs: Vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezing. SpO2 96% on room air.
Heart: Irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic, heart sounds of variable intensity, no murmurs. BP 148/90 mmHg. Pulse 115/min.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses or organomegaly. Peristalsis active.
Limbs: No pretibial edema, peripheral pulses palpable but irregular. No signs of deep vein thrombosis.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
ECG: Atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 118/min, normal axis, no acute ST-T wave changes, QRS duration 92ms.
    • WBC 7.4 [10⁹/L], RBC 4.21 [10¹²/L], Hb 132 g/L, Hct 0.390 L/L, MCV 92.6 fL, Platelets 245 [10⁹/L], NEU% 58.0%, LYM% 32.0%, Glucose 8.2 mmol/L, Urea 6.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 62 mL/min/1.73m², Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL (Normal <0.04), Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, TSH 1.45 mIU/L, INR 1.05.
(Dr. MARIĆ IVANA, M.Sc. Biochemist, 18.08.2024 10:15)
Chest X-ray (PA view):
No acute pleuropulmonary changes. Heart shadow is at the upper limit of normal size. Hila are vascular. No pleural effusion.
(Dr. HORVAT MARKO, MD, 18.08.2024 10:45)
Conclusion
The patient presented with new-onset atrial fibrillation. During the stay in the emergency department, rate control was initiated with a single dose of Metoprolol 25 mg orally, after which the heart rate decreased to 85-90/min. The patient remains in atrial fibrillation but is hemodynamically stable and asymptomatic at rest. 
Given the patient's age (74), the CHA2DS2-VASc score is calculated as 1. According to current guidelines, a score of 1 in this clinical context does not necessitate immediate oral anticoagulation. 
The patient is discharged with an increase in beta-blocker therapy.
Therapy: Metoprolol tartrate 25 mg 2x1.
Recommendation: Scheduled outpatient cardiology review and 24-hour Holter ECG.
In case of chest pain, neurological symptoms (weakness, speech difficulties), or severe breathlessness, report immediately to the emergency department.
(Dr. KNEŽEVIĆ ANTE, MD, 18.08.2024 12:30)
gold_reference: The physician incorrectly calculated the CHA2DS2-VASc score as 1. For this 74-year-old female patient with Hypertension and Diabetes, the score should actually be 4 (Age 65-74 = 1, Female sex = 1, Hypertension = 1, Diabetes = 1). A score of 4 carries a high risk of stroke and requires mandatory oral anticoagulation (e.g., DOAC or Warfarin).

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1952

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
J44.1 Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with (acute) exacerbation, unspecified
J18.9 Pneumonia, unspecified organism
I48.0 Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 155/92 mmHg; Pulse: 112/min (Irregular); SpO2: 88% (Room Air); Temp: 38.2°C; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Visit
Shortness of breath, productive cough, fever.
Anamnesis
History: The patient has a long-standing history of COPD (GOLD Stage III) diagnosed 15 years ago, associated with a 40-pack-year smoking history (quit 5 years ago). He also suffers from arterial hypertension and permanent atrial fibrillation. He had a previous hospitalization for COPD exacerbation 8 months ago.
Current Illness: The patient presents to the ED complaining of progressively worsening dyspnea over the last 3 days. He reports increased sputum production which has changed color from white to thick yellow-green. Since yesterday, he has felt feverish and fatigued. He has been using his rescue inhaler every 2 hours with minimal relief. Denies chest pain, palpitations, or leg swelling.
Functions: Reduced appetite due to dyspnea. Bowel movements regular. Urination normal.
Habits: Ex-smoker. Occasional alcohol.
Chronic Medications: Tiotropium (Spiriva) 18 mcg 1x1, Salbutamol p.r.n., Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Bisoprolol 2.5 mg 1x1, **Warfarin 5 mg (dose adjusted by INR clinic)**.
Allergies: Penicillin (Rash).
Status
Patient is alert, oriented x3, looks acute ill, tachypneic at rest with pursed-lip breathing. Uses accessory respiratory muscles.
Head and Neck: JVP not elevated. Mucous membranes somewhat dry. Pharynx not injected.
Lungs: Diffuse expiratory wheezing bilaterally. Coarse crackles and bronchial breathing audible in the right lower lobe. Prolonged expiratory phase.
Heart: Irregularly irregular rhythm (tachycardic). No murmurs or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, bowel sounds active. No hepatosplenomegaly.
Extremities: Warm, CRF < 2s. No peripheral edema. No signs of DVT.
Skin: Warm, dry, no rash.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
ECG: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (108-115 bpm). No acute ST-T wave changes. QTc 440ms.
Arterial Blood Gas (on 2L O2): pH 7.34, pCO2 6.1 kPa, pO2 8.8 kPa, HCO3 26 mmol/L, BE +1.2. (Mild respiratory acidosis).
Chest X-Ray: Hyperinflated lung fields consistent with COPD. New patch of consolidation in the right lower zone suggestive of pneumonia. No pneumothorax.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 16.5 [1e9]/L (Neutrophilia), Hgb 135 g/L, Plt 210 [1e9]/L.
Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Urea 8.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 105 µmol/L.
CRP 145 mg/L.
Troponin T < 14 ng/L.
**INR: 2.4** (Therapeutic range).
Therapy
Oxygen 2L via nasal cannula (target SpO2 88-92%).
Nebulized Salbutamol 2.5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg stat.
Solu-Medrol 40 mg IV stat.
Paracetamol 1g IV for fever.
**Clarithromycin 500 mg IV bid.**
Ceftriaxone 2g IV qd.
Conclusion
The patient is admitted to the Pulmonology Department for the management of infective exacerbation of COPD and Right Lower Lobe Pneumonia. Start IV antibiotics and steroids as charted. Continue chronic cardiac medications. Monitor respiratory status and O2 saturation continuously.
gold_reference: The patient is on Warfarin with a therapeutic INR of 2.4. Clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor and can significantly inhibit the metabolism of Warfarin. This interaction frequently leads to a rapid and dangerous increase in INR, causing a high risk of major bleeding. The report prescribes Clarithromycin without any note of dose reduction for Warfarin or increased monitoring, and ideally, a different antibiotic class should be chosen given the interaction.

**Medical History:**

**Current Illness:**  
- Woke at ~04:30 with sudden severe epigastric pain, constant, “boring” quality, radiating to the back.  
- Multiple episodes of non-bloody vomiting (5–6 times).  
- Reports the pain is worse when lying flat; minimal relief sitting forward.  
- No chest pain, no dyspnea.  
- No melena/hematemesis.  
- Last alcohol intake: “2 beers” two days ago; denies heavy alcohol use.  
- Last meal: fatty dinner the evening prior.

**Functions/Habits:**  
- Urination normal. No diarrhea.  
- Smokes 10 cigarettes/day.  
- Alcohol occasional.

**Medications:**  
- Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1  
- Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1 (intermittent adherence)  
- Ibuprofen PRN (took 400 mg once this morning without relief)

**Allergies:**  
- No known drug allergies (NKDA)

________________________________________  
## Physical Examination (09:05)
- **General:** Awake, oriented, in pain, holding upper abdomen. Mild dehydration (dry mucosa).  
- **Skin:** Warm, no jaundice.  
- **Lungs:** Vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles/wheezes.  
- **Heart:** Regular rhythm, tachycardic, no murmurs.  
- **Abdomen:** Soft, **marked tenderness in epigastrium**, mild RUQ tenderness, no rebound/guarding, bowel sounds present.  
- **Extremities:** No edema. Peripheral pulses palpable.  
- **Neuro:** No focal deficits.

**ECG (09:02):** Sinus tachycardia ~102/min, no ischemic ST-T changes.

________________________________________  
## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### Laboratory Results (blood drawn 09:12; reported 10:06)
**CBC:**  
- WBC: **12.8 ×10⁹/L** (↑)  
- Hemoglobin: 151 g/L  
- Platelets: 268 ×10⁹/L  

**Inflammation:**  
- CRP: **38 mg/L** (↑)

**Renal/Electrolytes:**  
- Urea: 7.1 mmol/L  
- Creatinine: 96 µmol/L  
- eGFR: 83 mL/min/1.73m²  
- Na: 134 mmol/L (low-normal)  
- K: 4.3 mmol/L  
- Cl: 98 mmol/L  
- HCO₃: 23 mmol/L  

**Liver tests:**  
- AST: **86 U/L** (↑)  
- ALT: **92 U/L** (↑)  
- ALP: 118 U/L  
- GGT: **210 U/L** (↑)  
- Total bilirubin: 18 µmol/L (upper-normal)

**Pancreatic enzymes:**  
- **Lipase: 986 U/L** (markedly ↑; reference < 60 U/L)  
- Amylase: **412 U/L** (↑)

**Glucose:** 8.9 mmol/L (↑ mild stress hyperglycemia)  
**hs-Troponin T:** 7 ng/L (normal)  

*(Lab validated by: M. Kovačević, Medical Biochemist, 06.02.2026 10:06)*

### Imaging
**Abdominal Ultrasound (11:20)**  
- Liver: mildly echogenic (steatosis).  
- Gallbladder: **multiple small gallstones**, no wall thickening, no pericholecystic fluid.  
- Common bile duct: 6 mm (upper-normal), no definite stone visualized.  
- Pancreas: partially obscured by bowel gas; no focal lesion described.  
- Free fluid: none.

*(Reported by: Dr. A. Horvat, Radiology, 06.02.2026 11:28)*

________________________________________  
## Therapy / ED Course
- IV line placed; monitoring initiated.  
- **0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV** over ~90 min.  
- **Ondansetron 4 mg IV** once.  
- **Pantoprazole 40 mg IV** once.  
- **Metamizole 2.5 g IV** once for analgesia.  

**Reassessment (12:15):** Pain decreased to VAS 4/10; no further vomiting in ED; vitals stable (BP 138/88, HR 92, T 37.3°C).

________________________________________  
## Conclusion / Disposition (12:40)
**“Findings most consistent with acute gastritis/indigestion after dietary trigger. No evidence of acute surgical abdomen. Discharge home with PPI therapy and diet modification.”**  

**Discharge Medications:**  
- Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily for 14 days  
- Ondansetron 4 mg PO every 8 hours PRN nausea (max 3/day)

**Recommendations:**  
- Oral hydration, light diet. Avoid NSAIDs and alcohol.  
- Follow up with family physician within 2–3 days.  
- Return immediately for worsening pain, persistent vomiting, fever, jaundice, or inability to tolerate fluids.

________________________________________
gold_reference: The patient has classic features and objective evidence of acute pancreatitis: severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, vomiting, and markedly elevated lipase (986 U/L, >3x upper limit of normal). Gallstones on ultrasound with elevated AST/ALT/GGT suggests possible gallstone pancreatitis. Discharging as gastritis overlooks a potentially serious diagnosis requiring monitoring and supportive care.

**Diagnoses**

**History:**

Personal history and previous illnesses: Patient between 70 and 75 years old is being treated for type 2 diabetes mellitus, BPH, hypothyroidism (total thyroidectomy performed due to medullary thyroid carcinoma). Functions and habits: good appetite, stable body weight for the last 6 months, urinates 2-3x at night. Stools are normal, without pathological admixtures. Has not smoked for 35 years, smoked for 20 years, 20 cigarettes/day. Drinks 2-3 dl of wine daily.

Nov 13: FENO: 55 ppb—elevated value.
Nov 13, 2024: Spirometry with BDT: FVC 95.5-93.5, FEV1 96.1-104.1, TI 77.52-82.46, MEF50: 95.6-127.9—values within reference ranges, BDT negative, but with improvement at the level of small airways after bronchodilation.
Dec 9, 2025: Spirometry with BDT: FVC 88-91% (3.44L), FEV1 89-96% (2.66L), TI 0.77-0.81, PEF 110-99%, FEF50 85-122% DLCO 86%, KCO 85%, VA 100%
Mar 6, 2025: Chest X-ray (PA and lateral profile): the pulmonary parenchyma available for analysis shows no convincing signs of fresh infiltrative changes. An oval shadow is visible basally on the left, diff. dg. nipple shadow. In case of clinical necessity, perform an X-ray with marked nipples. Hila are vascular, with normal terminal branching. The left diaphragmatic dome and corresponding costophrenic sinus are clear. The right diaphragmatic dome is positioned higher, lateral costophrenic sinus is shallower. Heart shadow size within physiological proportions. Status post sternotomy.

**Conclusion:**
I advise continuing the use of Alvesco now at a double dose and other chronic therapy as before. Plan a follow-up in 3 months with new findings: spirometry with Ventolin test, DLCO, FENO, chest X-ray (PA, lateral with marking of the nipples), lab findings (CBC, WBC differential, CRP, urea, creatinine, AST, ALT, GGT, Na, K, Cl, Ca, LDH, NT-proBNP, urine). ENT specialist examination and cardiologist examination are advised. Bring the inhaler to the follow-up (question of inhalation technique). Earlier follow-up according to clinical indication.
gold_reference: Alvesco double dose

report_content:
MBOO: 248771604

Date of Birth: 1952 (Male)

Triage Category: 2

---

## Diagnoses
- J18.9 Pneumonia, unspecified organism (community-acquired)
- J96.01 Acute respiratory failure with hypoxia
- A41.9 Sepsis, unspecified organism

---

## Triage Data
- Triage Category: 2  
- Chief complaint: Shortness of breath, fever, productive cough, confusion  
- Blood Pressure: 86/54 mmHg  
- Pulse: 118/min  
- Respirations: 32/min  
- Temperature: 39.1 °C  
- SpO₂: 87% on room air (improved to 94% on 4 L/min nasal cannula)  
- VAS: 3/10 (pleuritic discomfort)  
- GCS: 14 (E4 V4 M6; disoriented to time)

---

## History (Anamnesis)
**Past medical history:**  
- Hypertension (10+ years)  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (6 years)  
- COPD (former smoker, quit 5 years ago; ~35 pack-years)  
- Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline creatinine ~115 µmol/L per patient’s card)  

**Medications (home):**  
- Ramipril 5 mg 1×1  
- Amlodipine 5 mg 1×1  
- Metformin 1000 mg 1+0+1  
- Tiotropium inhaler 1×1  

**Allergies:** Denies known drug allergies.

**Present illness:**  
3 days of fever, chills, productive cough with yellow sputum, progressive dyspnea on minimal exertion. Family reports increasing confusion since morning. No chest pressure-type pain. No recent travel. No known sick contacts.

**Functions:** Reduced oral intake last 2 days. Urination decreased today. No vomiting or diarrhea.

**Habits:** Former smoker; no alcohol.

---

## Physical Examination (Status)
**General:** Ill-appearing, febrile, tachypneic, mildly dehydrated, confused but cooperative.  
**Skin:** Warm; capillary refill ~3 s.  
**HEENT:** Dry mucous membranes.  
**Lungs:** Reduced breath sounds and coarse crackles over right base; scattered wheeze. Increased work of breathing.  
**Heart:** Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no audible murmur.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender, no guarding; bowel sounds present.  
**Extremities:** No edema, no calf tenderness. Peripheral pulses palpable.  
**Neuro:** No focal deficit; attention impaired, slow responses.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### ECG
- Sinus tachycardia ~115/min  
- No ST-elevation; no acute ischemic changes

### Arterial Blood Gas (on 4 L/min nasal cannula)
- pH 7.45  
- pCO₂ 33 mmHg  
- pO₂ 66 mmHg  
- HCO₃⁻ 22 mmol/L  
- Lactate 3.2 mmol/L  

### Laboratory Results
**CBC**
- WBC 18.6 ×10⁹/L (Neutrophils 90%)  
- Hemoglobin 132 g/L  
- Platelets 168 ×10⁹/L  

**Inflammation**
- CRP 238 mg/L  
- Procalcitonin 6.1 ng/mL  

**Renal / Metabolic**
- Urea 12.4 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 156 µmol/L (above stated baseline)  
- eGFR ~40 mL/min/1.73m²  
- Sodium 131 mmol/L  
- Potassium 4.6 mmol/L  
- Glucose 14.8 mmol/L  

**Coagulation**
- INR 1.1  
- aPTT 28 s  

**Microbiology**
- Two sets of blood cultures drawn prior to antibiotics  
- Sputum sent for Gram stain/culture  
- Rapid influenza/COVID testing: negative (per ED rapid assay)

(Reported by: ED lab, 06.02.2026, 11:12)

---

## Imaging

### Chest X-ray (AP portable)
**Findings:**  
- Right lower lobe airspace opacity consistent with consolidation  
- Mild blunting of right costophrenic angle (small parapneumonic effusion cannot be excluded)  
- No pneumothorax  
- Cardiomediastinal silhouette within limitations of portable technique  

**Impression:** Right lower lobe pneumonia.

(Radiology report finalized: 06.02.2026, 11:28)

---

## Clinical Risk Assessment (contains clinical error)
**CURB-65 score calculated as 2 (moderate risk).**  
Documented rationale in chart: “Age >65 (1), urea >7 (1), others 0.”

---

## Therapy in ED
- Oxygen via nasal cannula 4 L/min → titrated to maintain SpO₂ ≥ 92%  
- IV access ×2, monitoring (ECG, SpO₂, BP)  
- 0.9% NaCl bolus 1000 mL (cautious due to CKD) → reassess perfusion and BP  
- Empiric antibiotics for severe CAP:
  - Ceftriaxone 2 g IV  
  - Azithromycin 500 mg IV  
- Paracetamol 1 g IV for fever  
- Nebulized bronchodilator (salbutamol/ipratropium) due to wheeze/COPD history  
- Repeat lactate ordered after fluids  
- Consider vasopressor support if persistent hypotension after fluids (sepsis pathway)

---

## Course in ED
After 1 L fluids, BP improved to 96/60 mmHg, HR 108/min, SpO₂ 94% on 4 L/min. Patient remained tachypneic (RR 28–30/min) with persistent confusion. Given hypotension, lactate elevation, hypoxemia, and radiographic pneumonia, treated as severe CAP with sepsis.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
- Diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia (RLL) with sepsis and acute hypoxemic respiratory failure; AKI on CKD suspected (prerenal component).  
- Plan: Hospital admission for close monitoring, IV antibiotics, oxygen therapy, sepsis management.  
- Destination: High-dependency/ICU evaluation due to initial hypotension, lactate elevation, and ongoing respiratory distress.  
- Follow-up orders: Repeat lactate, repeat BMP/renal function, strict I/O, reassess need for NIV/high-flow oxygen, review cultures and tailor antibiotics.

---
gold_reference: Based on the documented vitals/labs the CURB-65 components are: Confusion=1 (present), Urea >7 mmol/L=1 (12.4), Respiratory rate ≥30=1 (32), Low BP (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60)=1 (86/54), Age ≥65=1 (born 1952). Total should be 5, not 2. Underestimating the score understates severity and mortality risk.

Date of Birth: 1955

Diagnoses

N18.4 Chronic kidney disease, stage 4
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 165/95 mmHg; Pulse: 92/min; SpO2: 97%; Temp: 37.1°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Date	RR (mmHg)	Pulse (min)	SpO2 (%)	Resp (min)	Temp (°C)	Guk (mmol/L)	Vas	GCS
16.07.2024 09:15	168/96	94	97	18	37.1	6.8	8	15
16.07.2024 10:45	155/90	88	-	-	-	-	5	15
Reason for Arrival
Severe pain and swelling in the right big toe.
Anamnesis
Current Illness: Patient presents with acute onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling in the right first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint. Symptoms began early this morning, waking the patient from sleep. Reports inability to bear weight on the right foot. Denies trauma to the area. Denies fever or chills.
History: Patient has a history of arterial hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, and Chronic Kidney Disease (Stage 4, baseline Creatinine ~250 µmol/L). Last nephrology follow-up was 3 months ago.
Functions and Habits: Urination reported as slightly frothy, volume normal. Bowel movements regular. Former smoker (quit 10 years ago).
Medications: Amlodipine 10mg, Furosemide 40mg, Lantus Insulin, Allopurinol 100mg (patient admits to missing doses recently).
Allergies: Penicillin (Rash).
Status
Conscious, oriented x3. Osteomuscular build. Afebrile.
Head/Neck: Pupils equal and reactive. Mucous membranes moist. Neck veins flat.
Chest: Vesicular breathing, no adventitious sounds.
Heart: Rhythmic action, tones clear, no murmurs. Tachycardia consistent with pain response.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, peristalsis audible.
Extremities:
Right foot: Significant erythema, edema, and warmth localized to the 1st MTP joint. Extreme tenderness to palpation. Range of motion limited by pain. Dorsalis pedis pulse palpable. No signs of ascending lymphangitis.
Left foot: Unremarkable.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
X-ray Right Foot:
Soft tissue swelling noted around the 1st MTP joint. No fracture or dislocation visible. Mild degenerative changes consistent with age. No erosions typical of chronic tophaceous gout seen.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.10 [1e12]/L, HGB 125 g/L, Hct 0.380 L/L, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L
Biochemistry:
Blood Glucose 7.1 mmol/L
Urea 18.2 mmol/L (High)
Creatinine 268 µmol/L (High)
eGFR 21.4 mL/min/1.73m² (Low)
Uric Acid 545 µmol/L (High)
Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.8 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L
CRP 28 mg/L (High)
Therapy
Administered in ED:
    • 0.9% NaCl 250ml IV
    • Paracetamol 1000mg IV
    
Prescribed for home use:
    • Ibuprofen 600 mg film-coated tablets. Take 1 tablet orally every 8 hours with food for pain and inflammation.
    • Continue chronic therapy as previously prescribed.
Conclusion
Patient presents with clinical and laboratory findings consistent with an acute gouty flare of the right 1st MTP joint (Podagra), likely precipitated by missed Allopurinol doses.
X-ray rules out fracture.
Inflammatory markers are elevated. Renal function shows known chronic impairment (CKD 4).
Discharged home with anti-inflammatory analgesic therapy.
Control with family doctor in 3 days.
Report to ED if fever develops or pain becomes unmanageable.
gold_reference: The patient has documented Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 4 with a creatinine of 268 µmol/L and an eGFR of 21.4 mL/min/1.73m². NSAIDs (like Ibuprofen) are strictly contraindicated in patients with severe renal impairment (eGFR < 30) as they inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, reducing renal blood flow and potentially causing acute kidney injury or accelerating the progression to end-stage renal disease.

Diagnoses
F41.0 Panic disorder [episodic paroxysmal anxiety]
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; Blood Pressure: 118/72 mmHg; Pulse: 112/min; SpO2: 95%; Resp: 24/min; Temp: 36.8°C; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Date	RR (mmHg)	Pulse (min)	SpO2 (%)	Resp (min)
12.08.2024 18:45	118/72	112	95	24
12.08.2024 19:30	115/70	106	96	20
Reason for Visit
Palpitations, shortness of breath, feeling of "impending doom."
Anamnesis
Current Illness:
Patient presents complaining of sudden onset palpitations and shortness of breath starting 2 hours ago while studying for university exams. Reports feeling lightheaded and chest tightness. She states she has been sitting at her desk for 10-12 hours a day for the past 3 days.
She mentions a dull ache in her right calf since this morning, which she attributes to a muscle cramp from sitting awkwardly.
Personal History:
History of mild anxiety during exam periods.
Medications: Oral contraceptives (Yasmin) for 3 years.
Smoking: Occasional social smoker.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (Rash).
Status
Conscious, oriented x3, anxious, tachypneic at rest.
Head/Neck: Mucous membranes moist, no JVD.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or rales. Good air entry.
Heart: Tachycardic rhythm, regular, no murmurs/rubs/gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, bowel sounds present.
Extremities: Warm and dry. Right calf is slightly swollen (2cm > Left) and tender on palpation. Homan's sign equivocal. Peripheral pulses palpable.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
12-Channel ECG:
Sinus tachycardia, rate 110 bpm. Inverted T-waves in leads V1-V3. No ST-segment elevation.

Radiographic Imaging Chest PA:
Lung fields clear. No consolidation, pneumothorax, or pleural effusion. Cardiac silhouette normal size.

Laboratory Results:
WBC 9.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.45 [1e12]/L, HGB 135 g/L, Hct 0.40 L/L, Platelets 280 [1e9]/L
Na 139 mmol/L, K 3.9 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L, Urea 3.5 mmol/L, Creatinine 62 µmol/L
Troponin T hs: 4 ng/L (Normal < 14)
CK-MB: 2.1 ng/mL
CRP: 4.5 mg/L
D-dimer: 0.78 mg/L (Reference < 0.50 mg/L)
TSH: 1.4 mIU/L

Therapy
Diazepam 5mg PO given at 19:00.
500ml 0.9% NaCl IV.

Conclusion
Patient presents with symptoms consistent with acute anxiety/panic attack precipitated by exam stress.
ECG shows sinus tachycardia consistent with anxiety state. Troponin negative, ruling out acute myocardial infarction. Chest X-ray normal.
D-dimer mildly elevated, likely due to oral contraceptive use or mild inflammation, non-specific in this context.
Patient reports feeling calmer after Diazepam. Heart rate settled to 100 bpm.
Discharged home. Advised to rest and follow up with GP for anxiety management.
gold_reference: The physician diagnosed a Panic Attack but overlooked a likely Pulmonary Embolism (PE). The patient has significant risk factors (Oral contraceptive use, immobility/studying, smoking) and classic signs (Tachycardia, hypoxia/borderline SpO2, unilateral calf swelling/tenderness suggesting DVT). The D-dimer was elevated (0.78) and T-wave inversions in V1-V3 (right heart strain) were present on ECG. These findings were dismissed as anxiety.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1974

Triage Category: 4
Diagnoses
M10.0 Idiopathic gout
N18.4 Chronic kidney disease, stage 4
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension

Triage Data
Triage Cat: 4; BP: 155/95 mmHg; Pulse: 82/min; SpO2: 97%; Temp: 37.1°C; VAS: 8-Severe pain; GCS: 15

Vital Signs
BP: 155/95 mmHg, Pulse: 82/min, Temp: 37.1°C

Anamnesis
Current Illness: The patient presents with sudden, intense pain in the right big toe that began in the early morning hours. Describes the pain as "throbbing" and "unbearable," making it impossible to wear a shoe or walk. No history of recent trauma or injury to the foot. He notes similar but milder episodes in the past, but never this severe.
Past Illnesses: Chronic kidney disease (Stage 4, followed by a nephrologist at a regional center), arterial hypertension for 10 years, hyperuricemia.
Family History: Father had gout and kidney stones.
Functions: Urination is somewhat decreased in volume, no dysuria. Bowel movements regular.
Habits: Occasional alcohol consumption (beer), non-smoker.
Therapy: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Allopurinol 100 mg 1x1 (states he stopped taking it 2 days ago when he felt the "tingling" in his joint), Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1.
Allergies: Denies.

Status
Of medium musculoskeletal build, alert, communicative, oriented in time and space. Eupneic, afebrile.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally.
Heart: Regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. BP 155/95 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly.
Extremities: The right first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint is significantly swollen, erythematous, and warm to the touch. Extreme exquisite tenderness on palpation and any attempt at passive movement. No signs of cellulitis extending to the midfoot. Peripheral pulses (a. dorsalis pedis) palpable and symmetric.

EKG: Sinus rhythm, rate 80/min, normal axis, no acute ST-T changes.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [10⁹/L], RBC 3.90 [10¹²/L], Hb 118 g/L, Hct 0.350 L/L, MCV 89.7 fL, Platelets 240 [10⁹/L], NEU 78%, CRP 45.2 mg/L.
Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, Urea 16.5 mmol/L, **Creatinine 242 µmol/L**, **eGFR 23 mL/min/1.73m²**, Uric Acid 590 µmol/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.8 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
(DR. L. KNEŽEVIĆ, 22.07.2024 09:15)

X-ray of the Right Foot:
No signs of acute fracture or dislocation. Soft tissue swelling around the first MTP joint. Small "punched-out" erosions on the first metatarsal head, consistent with chronic gouty changes.
(Dr. J. Horvat, 22.07.2024 09:40)

Therapy
Administered in the Emergency Department:
- Voltaren (diclofenac) 75 mg i.m.
- Pantoprazole 40 mg i.v.

Conclusion
(Dr. MARIĆ IVAN, MD, 22.07.2024 10:30)
The patient presents with an acute gout flare-up (M10.0) involving the right first MTP joint. Laboratory findings confirm hyperuricemia and an inflammatory response (elevated CRP and WBC). Known history of Stage 4 CKD is noted with a current eGFR of 23 mL/min/1.73m².

Pain was partially relieved following intramuscular analgesic administration. The patient is discharged for home care.

Recommendations:
- Strict low-purine diet and hydration.
- Continue regular antihypertensive therapy.
- For pain management: Ibuprofen 600 mg p.o. every 8 hours for the next 3-5 days.
- Follow-up with a primary care physician and nephrologist.
- Do not restart Allopurinol until the acute flare has completely subsided.
gold_reference: The patient has Stage 4 Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) with an eGFR of 23 mL/min/1.73m². NSAIDs like Voltaren (diclofenac) and Ibuprofen are strictly contraindicated in patients with severe renal impairment (Stage 4 or 5) because they inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, leading to afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction, which can cause acute-on-chronic renal failure and further permanent decline in kidney function.

## Anamnesis

**History:**  
No prior myocardial infarction. No known thrombophilia. Mild asthma in childhood (no inhalers currently). No prior surgeries.

**Current illness:**  
Sudden onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain starting ~6 hours prior to arrival, worse with deep inspiration and coughing. ## Status (Physical Exam)
**General:** Alert, anxious-appearing, speaking in full sentences but mildly dyspneic. Afebrile.  
**Skin/mucosa:** Warm, well perfused. No cyanosis.

**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender, no guarding.

**Neuro:** No focal deficit.

---

## Course in ED / Reassessment
After analgesia, pain decreased to VAS 3–4/10. SpO₂ stable 96–97% on room air at rest, HR ~96/min. Patient reports improved breathing.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
**Assessment:** Chest pain is most consistent with **costochondritis with anxiety-related hyperventilation**, given reproducible chest wall tenderness, normal ECG, normal troponin, and unremarkable chest X-ray.  

**Plan:** Discharge home. Continue ibuprofen 400 mg every 8 hours as needed with food for 3 days. Rest, hydration. Follow up with primary care physician within 2–3 days.

**Return precautions:** Worsening shortness of breath, persistent tachycardia, syncope, hemoptysis, increasing chest pain, new leg swelling.

**Discharge statement (contains the clinical error):**  
> “Despite elevated D-dimer, there is no indication for further imaging; pulmonary embolism is unlikely and does not require CT pulmonary angiography at this time.”

(ED physician: 06.02.2026 22:05)

---
gold_reference: The patient has multiple PE risk features (recent long-haul flight, estrogen-containing OCP), symptoms compatible with PE (sudden pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, tachycardia, initial borderline hypoxemia), and an elevated D-dimer. Declaring PE unlikely without applying a validated pretest probability tool (e.g., Wells/YEARS/PERC) and without appropriate imaging can miss a potentially life-threatening diagnosis.

- Normal bowel movement yesterday.  
- Smokes: no. Alcohol: occasional.

Allergies:

Status  
General: In acute distress from pain, restless, mobile, oriented, afebrile, well perfused, not icteric.  
Lungs: Vesicular breath sounds bilaterally, no wheeze/crackles. SpO2 99% on room air.  
Heart: Regular rhythm, tachycardic initially, no murmurs.  
Abdomen: Soft, not distended. No peritoneal signs. Mild tenderness left lower quadrant without guarding.

- Laboratory tests: CBC, CRP, electrolytes, renal function, liver enzymes, glucose; urinalysis.  
- Imaging: Ultrasound of kidneys and urinary tract (bedside/radiology).

Findings and Opinion  

Ultrasound of kidneys and urinary tract  
- Right kidney: normal size and echogenicity, no hydronephrosis.  
- Left kidney: mild pelvicalyceal dilatation (mild hydronephrosis).  
- Ureter: distal ureter not reliably visualized; no definite stone seen on US.  
- Urinary bladder: adequately filled, no mass, no significant post-void residual (limited by pain).  

Impression: Indirect signs consistent with left-sided ureteral obstruction, most likely ureterolithiasis. No ultrasound signs of pyonephrosis.  

Conclusion (Lab)  
(CBC/biochemistry/urinalysis, 06.02.2026)  
- WBC 10.8 [1e9]/L  
- RBC 5.02 [1e12]/L  
- HGB 151 g/L  
- Hct 0.452 L/L

- Creatinine 92 µmol/L  
- eGFR 102 mL/min/1.73m²  
- Na 140 mmol/L  
- K 4.1 mmol/L  
- Cl 102 mmol/L  

Liver/pancreas:  
- AST 22 U/L, ALT 19 U/L, GGT 18 U/L, ALP 76 U/L  
- Total bilirubin 12 µmol/L  
- Lipase 24 U/L  
- Glucose 5.8 mmol/L  

Urinalysis:  
- Appearance: clear; color: yellow  
- pH 6.0; specific gravity 1.020  
- Leukocyte esterase: negative  
- Nitrite: negative  
- Protein: negative  
- Glucose: negative; ketones: trace  
- Blood: 3+  
Microscopy:  
- Erythrocytes 50–80 / HPF  
- Leukocytes 0–3 / HPF  
- Bacteria: none seen  
- Crystals: occasional calcium oxalate crystals  

Interpretation: Microscopic hematuria without evidence of urinary tract infection; renal function preserved; no significant inflammatory response.  

Therapy (ED)  
- IV access, monitoring.  
- 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV.  
- Metamizole 2.5 g IV slow.  
- Ketorolac 30 mg IV (after confirming no renal impairment/bleeding risk and no NSAID allergy).  
- Ondansetron 4 mg IV.  
- Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO in ED (medical expulsive therapy).  

Clinical Course / Observation  
After analgesia and fluids, pain decreased from VAS 8/10 to 2–3/10. Nausea resolved, no further vomiting. Hemodynamically stable and afebrile throughout. Able to tolerate oral fluids prior to discharge.  

Discharge Conclusion  
Clinical presentation (typical colicky flank pain radiating to groin), urinalysis with significant microscopic hematuria, and ultrasound showing mild left hydronephrosis are consistent with left ureterolithiasis without signs of infection or renal failure. Currently no indication for emergency hospitalization.

Discharge Plan / Recommendations  
1. Medications:  
   - Tamsulosin 0.4 mg: 1 capsule nightly for 14 days.  
   - Ibuprofen 400 mg: 1 tablet every 8 hours as needed for pain with food (max 1200 mg/day OTC equivalent), unless contraindicated.  
   - If pain not controlled: metamizole (if available/previously tolerated per local policy) as prescribed.  
   - Ondansetron 4 mg ODT/PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea (up to 2 days).  

2. Hydration: Maintain adequate oral fluids (aim for pale urine), avoid overhydration if it worsens pain.  

3. Stone capture: Use urine strainer; if stone passed, keep for analysis.  

4. Follow-up:  
   - Urology outpatient follow-up within 3–7 days.  
   - Repeat renal ultrasound or low-dose non-contrast CT KUB if pain persists, stone not passed, or recurrent episodes.  

5. Return immediately to ED if any of the following occur:  
   - Fever ≥ 38°C, chills, or feeling unwell (concern for obstructed infected system).  
   - Uncontrolled pain or persistent vomiting despite medications.  
   - Decreased urine output/anuria.  
   - New severe weakness, syncope, or gross hematuria with clots.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1971

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
K29.7 Gastritis, unspecified
R10.1 Pain localized to upper abdomen
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 108/64 mmHg; Pulse: 55/min; SpO2: 97%; Temp: 36.8°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Anamnesis
Past Medical History: Arterial hypertension for 10 years, treated with Amlodipine. Hypercholesterolemia. No history of coronary artery disease. Chronic smoker (25 pack-years).
Current Illness: Patient presented to the emergency department complaining of sudden onset, severe "burning" pain in the epigastrium that began approximately 90 minutes ago after eating a spicy meal. The pain radiates slightly to the lower chest and neck. He reports significant nausea and one episode of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. He denies fever, cough, or changes in bowel habits.
Therapy: Amlodipine 5 mg, Atorvastatin 20 mg.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status
General Status: Middle-aged male, appearing acutely ill, moderately diaphoretic (sweating), pale. Alert and oriented x3.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Bradycardic, regular rhythm, S1 and S2 clear, no murmurs or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, deep tenderness on palpation in the epigastric region. No guarding or rebound tenderness. Liver and spleen not palpable. Normal bowel sounds.
Extremities: Cool to the touch peripherally, no edema, pulses palpable but weak.
ECG: Sinus bradycardia, rate 54/min. 1.5mm ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. Subtle ST-segment depression in leads I and aVL.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.90 [1e12]/L, HGB 151 g/L, Hct 0.44 L/L, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, Glucose 7.8 mmol/L, Urea 6.4 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, CRP 2.1 mg/L.
Troponin T (hs-cTnT): 0.048 ng/mL (Reference range: <0.014 ng/mL).
AST 48 U/L, ALT 35 U/L, GGT 22 U/L, ALP 65 U/L, Amylase 58 U/L.
(Dr. Ivan Horvat, 22.10.2024 19:45)

Summary Image of Thoracic Organs:
No evidence of acute pulmonary infiltrates or congestion. Heart size at the upper limit of normal. Mediastinum appears normal.
(Dr. Marko Ivić, 22.10.2024 20:10)

Abdominal Ultrasound:
No evidence of cholecystitis or cholelithiasis. Pancreas appears normal in size and echogenicity. No free intraperitoneal fluid visualized.
(Dr. Ana Babić, 22.10.2024 20:30)
Therapy
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
Metoclopramide 10 mg IV
"GI Cocktail" (30mL Antacid + 10mL Lidocaine 2%) administered orally
Conclusion
Patient reports significant improvement in epigastric pain (VAS reduced from 8 to 2) following the administration of the GI cocktail and IV pantoprazole. The clinical picture is highly suggestive of acute gastritis exacerbated by dietary triggers. ECG shows sinus bradycardia and minor repolarization changes, likely chronic or secondary to the acute abdominal distress. Minimal troponin elevation is noted, possibly due to demand or analytical variation given the resolution of symptoms with GI therapy.
Discharged home.
Recommendations:
- Control with primary care physician in 2 days.
- Pantoprazole 40 mg once daily for 14 days.
- Bland diet.
- Return to ED if chest pain recurs or worsens.
(Dr. Stjepan Radić, MD, 22.10.2024 21:15)
gold_reference: The physician missed an Inferior Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). The patient had classic 'red flags': epigastric pain in a smoker with hypertension, diaphoresis, bradycardia, and an ECG showing ST elevation in II, III, and aVF with reciprocal changes in I and aVL. Additionally, the high-sensitivity Troponin T was elevated above the reference range. The physician incorrectly attributed the pain relief from a 'GI cocktail' as confirmation of a gastrointestinal cause, a common cognitive bias (premature closure).

No indication for urgent hospitalization in the Internal Medicine Clinic at this time. An echocardiogram and 24-hour Holter ECG monitoring are advised. Follow-up with a cardiologist for results and to decide on the need for anticoagulant therapy as per cardiologist's recommendation. Given the high risk of thromboembolic events, it is advised to initiate anticoagulant therapy—Martefarin or NOAC as agreed with the patient and the chosen family physician. Regular control of TSH, lipid profile, and blood pressure monitoring. Dabigatran Viatris 150 mg 2x1.

Senior service consulted. The evaluation showed no indication for urgent hospitalization. Due to the unknown duration of atrial fibrillation, conversion to sinus rhythm was not performed, and only rate control was recommended. Given the low thromboembolic risk (CHADs-VASc: 1, HAS-BLED: 1), it is advised to take Concor 5 mg 1x1 (if heart rate exceeds 110/min, increase dose to a maximum of 10 mg with blood pressure monitoring) for at least 4 weeks, after which a referral to the Cardiology Day Hospital (no. 557-253) for potential electrocardioversion is recommended.

Diagnoses  
- I21.4 Non–ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI)  
- I10 Essential (primary) hypertension  
- E78.5 Hyperlipidaemia, unspecified  

---

## Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 168/96 mmHg; Pulse: 102/min; SpO2: 96%; Temp: 36.8°C; Resp: 20/min; VAS: 7/10 (chest pain); GCS: 15;

## Vital Signs (ED Course)
| Date/Time | BP (mmHg) | Pulse (/min) | SpO2 (%) | Resp (/min) | Temp (°C) | Pain (VAS) | GCS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 06.02.2026 14:12 | 168/96 | 102 | 96 | 20 | 36.8 | 7 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 15:05 | 154/90 | 94 | 97 | 18 | 36.7 | 3 | 15 |
| 06.02.2026 17:30 | 150/88 | 88 | 97 | 16 | 36.7 | 1 | 15 |

---

## Reason for Arrival
Central chest pressure radiating to left arm with nausea and sweating.

---

## Anamnesis
**Current illness:**  
Patient reports 2 hours of retrosternal pressure (“tight band”), radiating to the left arm and jaw, associated with diaphoresis and nausea. Pain began while walking upstairs, partially improved with rest but did not fully resolve. No syncope. Mild dyspnea during the episode. No fever or cough.

**Past medical history:**  
Arterial hypertension (poorly controlled), hyperlipidemia. No known coronary artery disease. No prior MI. No known diabetes.

**Family history:**  
Father died of “heart attack” at 62.

**Functions/Habits:**  
Normal stool/urination. Smokes ~15 cigarettes/day for ~35 years. Occasional alcohol.

**Medications:**  
Amlodipine 5 mg daily (often missed), Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly (irregular). No anticoagulants.

**Allergies:**  
No known drug allergies.

---

## Status (Physical Examination)
Middle-aged male, anxious but oriented, skin slightly clammy.  
- **Airway:** patent  
- **Breathing:** eupneic at rest, lungs clear bilaterally, no wheeze/crackles  
- **Circulation:** warm extremities, capillary refill <2 s  
- **Heart:** regular rhythm, tachycardic initially, no murmurs  
- **Abdomen:** soft, non-tender  
- **Extremities:** no edema, peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric  
- **Neuro:** no focal deficits  

---

## ECG
**12-lead ECG (14:20):** Sinus tachycardia 105/min. Horizontal ST-segment depression ~1 mm in V4–V6 with T-wave inversion in aVL. No ST-elevation.  
**Repeat ECG (16:10):** Sinus rhythm 92/min. Persistent lateral ST depression ~0.5–1 mm.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

### Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (AP)
**Findings/Opinion:**  
No focal consolidation or pleural effusion. Mild cardiomegaly. No pneumothorax.  
(Dr. N. Kovač, 06.02.2026 14:55)

---

## Laboratory Results
(Performed 06.02.2026)

### Hematology
WBC 10.8 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.92 [1e12]/L, HGB 151 g/L, Hct 0.447 L/L, MCV 90.9 fL, Platelets 238 [1e9]/L.

### Biochemistry
Glucose 6.9 mmol/L, Urea 7.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 98 µmol/L, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 103 mmol/L, Mg 0.74 mmol/L, CRP 2.1 mg/L.

### Cardiac markers (high-sensitivity Troponin T)
- **hs-TnT (14:40): 128 ng/L** (ref <14)  
- **hs-TnT (17:05): 186 ng/L** (rising)

### Coagulation
INR 1.02, APTT 28.6 s, D-dimer 0.38 mg/L.

(Lab verified by M. Šarić, Medical Biochemist, 06.02.2026 17:20)

---

## ED Therapy / Procedures
- Aspirin 300 mg p.o. given in ED  
- Nitroglycerin spray 0.4 mg s.l. x2 (pain decreased from VAS 7 → 3)  
- Metoprolol 5 mg i.v. once (HR improved)  
- Ondansetron 4 mg i.v. once  
- Oxygen not required (SpO2 maintained ≥96%)  
- IV line placed, monitoring performed

---

## Clinical Impression
Chest pain typical for acute coronary syndrome with ischemic ECG changes and dynamic rise of troponin consistent with **NSTEMI**.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Patient informed of findings. Chest pain resolved at rest after nitroglycerin and beta-blocker. Hemodynamically stable.

**DISCHARGE PLAN (as documented):**  
**“Patient discharged home from the Emergency Department with advice to follow up with outpatient cardiology within 2–3 days. Continue atorvastatin. Start pantoprazole 40 mg daily for 14 days. Return if pain recurs.”**

---
gold_reference: The report documents NSTEMI features: typical ischemic chest pain, ischemic ECG changes (ST depression), and a dynamic rise in hs-troponin (128→186 ng/L). NSTEMI requires hospital admission for continuous monitoring, serial ECG/troponins, antithrombotic therapy, and cardiology evaluation for early invasive strategy. Discharging home risks recurrent ischemia, arrhythmia, cardiogenic shock, or sudden death.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1949

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
R07.4 Chest pain, unspecified
K21 Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
N18.3 Chronic kidney disease, stage 3
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 165/92 mmHg; Pulse: 88/min; SpO2: 95%; Temp: 36.7°C; VAS: 6 - Moderate pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Retrosternal and epigastric burning sensation.
Anamnesis
History: Long-standing arterial hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus managed with oral hypoglycemics, and known Chronic Kidney Disease (baseline creatinine approx. 125-135 µmol/L). Previous smoker (30 pack-years, quit 5 years ago). No previous history of coronary interventions.
Current Illness: Patient reports a burning sensation in the "pit of the stomach" and behind the breastbone that started approximately 4 hours ago while resting. The pain radiates slightly toward the neck. He describes it as similar to previous episodes of "heartburn" but notes it is more intense today. Denies nausea, vomiting, or diaphoresis. No shortness of breath at rest.
Functions: Stool and urination regular.
Habits: Does not consume alcohol.
Therapy: Ramipril 5 mg, Metformin 1000 mg, Amlodipine 5 mg, Pantoprazole 20 mg.
Allergies: Penicillin.
Status
Medium musculoskeletal build, slightly overweight, conscious, oriented, eupneic at rest, afebrile. Skin and visible mucous membranes of normal color.
Lungs: Normal vesicular breathing, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Regular rhythm, tones somewhat muffled, no murmurs. BP 158/89 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, mild tenderness on deep palpation in the epigastrium, no signs of peritoneal irritation. Liver and spleen not palpable.
Extremities: No edema, peripheral pulses palpable and symmetrical.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
EKG (Initial): Sinus rhythm, rate 84/min, intermediate electrical axis, 0.5 mm ST-segment depression in leads V5-V6, non-specific T-wave flattening.
EKG (3-hour control): Sinus rhythm, rate 82/min, persistent ST-segment depression in V5-V6, no new Q-waves.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 9.4 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.12 [1e12]/L, HGB 128 g/L, Hct 0.388 L/L, MCV 94.2 fL, MCH 31.1 pg, Platelets 198 [1e9]/L, NEU% 68.2 %, Glucose 8.4 mmol/L, Urea 9.1 mmol/L, Creatinine 138 µmol/L, eGFR 46.4 mL/min/1.73m², AST 32 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, CRP 5.2 mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.4 mmol/L.
High-sensitivity Troponin T (hs-TNT) at 0h: 18.4 ng/L (Reference <14 ng/L).
High-sensitivity Troponin T (hs-TNT) at 3h: 54.2 ng/L.
(Dr. Ivan Brkić, 22.08.2024 10:15)
Summary Image of Thoracic Organs, PA view:
No signs of acute pulmonary congestion or infiltrative changes. Heart shadow slightly enlarged to the left. Calcified aortic arch.
(Dr. Marija Šimunović, 22.08.2024 09:40)
Therapy
Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
Gaviscon liquid 20 mL PO
Aspirin 100 mg PO
Conclusion
The patient's chest pain significantly improved following the administration of oral antacids and IV pantoprazole, suggesting a gastrointestinal origin (GERD). While the high-sensitivity Troponin T is elevated, this is interpreted as a chronic elevation secondary to the patient's Stage 3 Chronic Kidney Disease, as the values remain relatively low in absolute terms. EKG changes are non-specific and stable. The patient is currently asymptomatic and hemodynamically stable. 
Discharged home.
Recommendations: Continue current antihypertensive therapy. Increase Pantoprazole to 40 mg once daily for 14 days. Schedule an outpatient treadmill stress test or cardiology consultation within the next 2 weeks. If chest pain recurs and is not relieved by antacids, return to the emergency department.
gold_reference: The patient has a significant 'delta' (change) in high-sensitivity Troponin T, rising from 18.4 ng/L to 54.2 ng/L over 3 hours. A rise of this magnitude (nearly 200%) in the context of chest pain and ST-segment depression on EKG is diagnostic of a Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI). The clinician incorrectly attributed the elevation to chronic kidney disease (CKD). While CKD causes chronically elevated baseline troponin, it does not cause an acute threefold rise within 3 hours. Furthermore, symptom relief with antacids (the 'GI cocktail' response) is notoriously unreliable for ruling out cardiac ischemia.

Reason for Arrival

Within the next 24 h he noted 2–3 episodes of melena. Over the past 12 h he developed light‑headedness on standing, orthostatic hypotension, fatigue, and dyspnea on minimal exertion. No abdominal pain, hematochezia, fever, recent NSAID binge, or alcohol excess. No prior similar episodes.  

Functions: Bowel – melena documented on rectal exam; no constipation or diarrhea. Urination – normal frequency and volume, adequate output.  

Habits: Former smoker (20 pack‑years, quit 10 y ago). Alcohol – 1–2 drinks/week, no binge drinking. No illicit drug use.  

Medications: Warfarin 5 mg PO daily; Aspirin 81 mg PO daily; Metformin 1000 mg PO BID; Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily; Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily; Atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily; Calcium carbonate (TUMS) 500 mg PO PRN; Ibuprofen 200 mg PO PRN (≈2 tablets weekly).  

Allergies: Penicillin – rash (hives).  

Status  
General: Pale, mildly diaphoretic, alert, oriented ×3, in no acute distress.  
HEENT: Conjunctival pallor; no icterus; oropharynx moist.  
Cardiovascular: Regular rhythm, tachycardic (112 bpm), no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; no jugular venous distension.  
Respiratory: Breath sounds clear bilaterally, no wheezes or rales; respiratory effort normal.  
Abdomen: Soft, non‑tender, mild epigastric fullness, no guarding or rebound; no organomegaly.  
Genitourinary: Rectal exam – dark, tarry stool consistent with melena; no gross blood. No suprapubic tenderness.  
Extremities: No peripheral edema; capillary refill <2 s supine, >3 s standing; peripheral pulses palpable.  
Neurologic: No focal deficits; cranial nerves intact; GCS 15.  

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations  
ECG: Sinus rhythm, rate 112 bpm, left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria, no ST‑segment changes, no new arrhythmias.  

Laboratory Results:  
- Hemoglobin: 9.2 g/dL (13.5–17.5)  
- Hematocrit: 27 % (41–53)  
- RBC: 3.2 ×10⁶/µL  
- MCV: 84 fL  
- MCH: 28 pg  
- WBC: 9.5 ×10⁹/L (4.0–11.0)  
- Platelets: 210 ×10⁹/L (150–400)  
- BUN: 48 mg/dL (7–20)  
- Creatinine: 1.9 mg/dL (0.6–1.3)  
- eGFR: 38 mL/min/1.73 m²  
- BUN/Creatinine ratio: 25.3  
- Sodium: 138 mmol/L (135–145)  
- Potassium: 4.6 mmol/L (3.5–5.0)  
- Chloride: 102 mmol/L (98–107)  
- Bicarbonate: 24 mmol/L (22–28)  
- Glucose: 110 mg/dL (70–110)  
- Calcium: 9.0 mg/dL (8.5–10.5)  
- Albumin: 3.8 g/dL (3.5–5.0)  
- Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL (0.1–1.2)  
- AST: 28 U/L (10–40)  
- ALT: 32 U/L (7–56)  
- Alkaline phosphatase: 78 U/L (44–147)  
- INR: 3.2 (therapeutic 2–3)  
- PT: 35 sec (11–15)  
- aPTT: 38 sec (25–35)  
- Troponin I: <0.01 ng/mL (<0.04)  
- Lactate: 1.8 mmol/L (0.5–2.2)  
- Type & cross‑match: O‑negative, 2 units reserved  

Imaging:  
- FAST (abdominal): No free intraperitoneal fluid.  
- Chest X‑ray: Clear lung fields, normal cardiac silhouette, no mediastinal widening.  
- CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast: No active contrast extravasation; mildly distended stomach with fluid; no perforation, mass, or aortic aneurysm.  
- Upper endoscopy (EGD): Pending; gastroenterology consult placed for urgent scope within 12 h.  

Consultations: Gastroenterology (urgent EGD), Hematology (warfarin reversal guidance), Cardiology (AF management post‑bleed).  

Therapy Administered in ED  
- 2 L normal saline IV bolus (0‑30 min) followed by 1 L NS over 30 min.  
- Pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus, then continuous infusion 8 mg/h.  
- Vitamin K 10 mg IV slow push.  
- 4‑factor prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) 1800 IU IV (≈25 IU/kg).  
- Packed red blood cells (O‑negative) 2 units transfused.  
- Ondansetron 4 mg IV (anti‑emetic).  
- Acetaminophen 650 mg PO PRN for discomfort.  
- Supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula 2 L/min (SpO₂ ≥ 96 %).  
- NPO status maintained.  
- Continuous cardiac telemetry, serial vitals q15 min, urine output monitoring.  
- Foley catheter not placed; bladder scan performed – adequate volume.  

Conclusion  
The patient presents with a high‑risk acute upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage (Glasgow‑Blatchford Score ≈ 13) in the setting of supratherapeutic warfarin anticoagulation (INR 3.2) and chronic kidney disease. Prompt resuscitation with crystalloid, blood product transfusion, rapid reversal of anticoagulation (IV vitamin K and PCC), and high‑dose IV proton‑pump inhibitor therapy was instituted. Post‑resuscitation he is hemodynamically stable (BP 130/80 mmHg, HR 88 bpm) with rising hemoglobin (post‑transfusion 9.8 g/dL). He will be admitted to the ICU for continued monitoring, repeat laboratory assessment, and urgent upper endoscopy within the next 12 hours for definitive hemostasis. **Warfarin will be held initially; CHA2DS2‑VASc score calculated as 2, indicating low stroke risk, so anticoagulation can be resumed on hospital day 2 without bridging.** Discharge planning will include gastroenterology follow‑up, anticoagulation clinic review, and patient education on medication adherence and signs of re‑bleeding.  

Prepared by: Dr. Ana Kovač, MD – Emergency Medicine  
Date: 02/02/2026  

---
gold_reference: The CHA2DS2‑VASc score for this 69‑year‑old male with hypertension, diabetes, and prior myocardial infarction (vascular disease) should be 4, not 2. Misclassifying the patient as low‑risk for stroke may lead to premature resumption of anticoagulation, increasing the risk of recurrent gastrointestinal bleeding. The correct score indicates a high stroke risk, requiring careful timing of anticoagulation re‑initiation and possible bridging after hemostasis.

Date of Birth: 1979

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
N20.1 Calculus of ureter
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; BP: 158/92 mmHg; Pulse: 104/min; SpO2: 99%; Temp: 36.8°C; VAS: 9 - Severe pain; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Date	BP (mmHg)	Pulse (min)	SpO2 (%)	Temp (°C)	VAS	GCS
24.05.2024 08:15	158/92	104	99	36.8	9	15
24.05.2024 10:45	135/82	84	-	-	2	15
Reason for Visit
Severe right-sided flank pain radiating to the groin.
Anamnesis
Current Illness: Patient presents with sudden onset of sharp, colicky pain in the right lumbar region, radiating towards the right inguinal area and testicle. Onset approximately 2 hours ago. Reports two episodes of vomiting due to pain intensity. Denies dysuria, urgency, or fever.
Past History: History of nephrolithiasis (last episode 5 years ago, passed spontaneously). Hypertension controlled on monotherapy.
Chronic Therapy: Ramipril 5mg.
Drug Allergies: Penicillin (Rash).
Status
General: Patient is alert, oriented, eupneic, but visibly distressed, restless, and unable to find a comfortable position ("kidney stone dance"). Afebrile.
Head and Neck: Mucous membranes slightly dry. No lymphadenopathy.
Lungs: Vesicular breathing, clear bilaterally.
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Bowel sounds present. Deep tenderness noted in the right lower quadrant. No rebound tenderness or guarding (negative Blumberg).
Back: Positive Giordano sign (costovertebral angle tenderness) on the right side. Left side painless.
Extremities: No edema, pulses equal and palpable.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
Urine Analysis (Dipstick & Sediment):
SG 1.025; pH 6.0; LEU 1+; NIT Neg; PRO Neg; GLU Neg; KET 1+; UBG Norm; BIL Neg; ERY 3+
Sediment: RBC 30-40/hpf; WBC 4-6/hpf; Mucus present; Bacteria rare.
Hematology - Complete Blood Count:
WBC 11.5↗ (1e9/L); RBC 5.10 (1e12/L); Hgb 148 g/L; Hct 0.44 L/L; MCV 88 fL; Plt 280 (1e9/L).
Biochemistry:
Urea 6.1 mmol/L; Creatinine 102↗ µmol/L; eGFR 82 mL/min; CRP 2.1 mg/L; Na 139 mmol/L; K 4.1 mmol/L; Cl 102 mmol/L.
Native CT of Abdomen and Pelvis (Low Dose Stone Protocol):
Findings:
Liver, spleen, pancreas, and adrenal glands are unremarkable.
Right Kidney: Normal size and parenchymal thickness. Grade II hydronephrosis and hydroureter visible.
Ureters: A hyperdense calculus measuring approximately 5x4 mm is visualized in the distal right ureter, at the ureterovesical junction (UVJ).
Left Kidney: No calculi or hydronephrosis.
Bladder: Distended, wall smooth.
Appendix: Normal visualization, no signs of inflammation.
Conclusion: Right distal ureteral lithiasis with secondary obstruction and moderate hydronephrosis.
(Dr. A. Petrovic, Radiologist, 24.05.2024 09:30)
Therapy
    • 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV
    • Diclofenac 75 mg IM
    • Metoclopramide 10 mg IV
    • Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
Conclusion
Patient presented with classic renal colic symptoms. Diagnosis of right distal ureteral stone (5mm) confirmed by CT. No signs of urinary tract infection or compromised renal function requiring urgent intervention (creatinine only mildly elevated due to obstruction, no fever, WBC mildly elevated likely due to stress/pain).
Following administration of analgesia and fluids, the patient reports significant relief (VAS dropped to 2/10).
Discharged home with conservative management (MET - Medical Expulsive Therapy).
Recommendations:
1. Hydration (2-3 L of water daily).
2. Tamsulosin 0.4 mg 1x1 daily for 2 weeks to facilitate stone passage.
3. Diclofenac 50 mg orally every 8 hours as needed for pain.
4. Strain urine to catch stone for analysis.
5. Urology outpatient follow-up in 7-10 days with control ultrasound.
6. Return to ED immediately if fever >38°C, intractable pain, or inability to urinate occurs.
gold_reference: No errors

Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; BP: 118/76 mmHg; Pulse: 114/min; SpO2: 91% (Room Air); Temp: 37.2°C; RR: 26/min; VAS: 6-Moderate/Severe pain; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Anamnesis
Current Illness: Patient presents with sudden onset of shortness of breath and sharp, right-sided pleuritic chest pain that began approximately three hours ago while resting. Reports a "heavy" feeling in the left leg for the past 3 days. Denies cough, hemoptysis, or syncope.
Past Illnesses: Total left hip arthroplasty 14 days ago. Essential hypertension.
Family History: Father had a myocardial infarction at age 62.
Functions: Normal urination, regular bowel movements.
Habits: Smoker (10 cigarettes/day for 20 years). Does not consume alcohol.
Therapy: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Acetylsalicylic acid 100 mg 1x1 (discontinued 3 days ago per patient's own decision).
Allergies: Denies.
Status
Musculoskeletal build: Average, alert, communicative, oriented in time and space, tachypneic, mildly distressed, afebrile. Skin and visible mucous membranes are pale, no cyanosis.
Lungs: Auscultation reveals clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. SpO2 91% on room air, improves to 97% on 2L O2 via nasal cannula.
Heart: Tachycardic rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. S1Q3T3 pattern not clearly visible. BP: 118/76 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly, audible peristalsis.
Limbs: Left lower leg is visibly edematous compared to the right (circumference +3.5 cm at the calf). Tenderness on deep palpation of the left calf. Homans' sign positive on the left. Peripheral pulses palpable.
EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 115/min, normal axis, inverted T waves in V1-V3.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
Laboratory Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.80 [1e12]/L, HGB 145 g/L, Hct 0.420 L/L, MCV 87.5 fL, Trc 210 [1e9]/L, D-dimer 3450 ng/mL (Ref: <500), Troponin T (hs-TnT) 48 ng/L (Ref: <14), NT-proBNP 950 pg/mL (Ref: <300), CRP 12.4 mg/L, Glucose 6.2 mmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 82 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, AST 32 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, LDH 280 U/L.
(LUKIC ANA mag.med.biochem., 22.08.2024 14:15)

Arterial Blood Gas (on 2L O2):
pH 7.46, pCO2 4.2 kPa, pO2 10.8 kPa, HCO3 22.4 mmol/L, BE -1.2.

Radiographic Imaging:
Chest X-Ray (AP): No infiltrates, no pleural effusion. Heart shadow within normal limits. Normal pulmonary vascularity.

CT Angiography of the Pulmonary Arteries (CTPA):
Filling defects observed in the right main pulmonary artery and the segmental branches of the right lower lobe, consistent with acute pulmonary embolism. No evidence of right ventricular enlargement (RV/LV ratio < 1.0).

Color Doppler Ultrasound of Lower Extremity Veins:
Non-compressible segment of the left popliteal vein and posterior tibial veins with absence of flow, consistent with acute deep vein thrombosis (DVT).
Dr. M. Horvat

Conclusion:
(Dr. MARIĆ IVAN, MD, 22.08.2024 16:30)
Patient presented with classic symptoms of pulmonary embolism (Wells Score 9.0: HR > 100, recent surgery, clinical signs of DVT, and PE being the most likely diagnosis). Diagnosis confirmed via CTPA and elevated D-dimer. Elevated Troponin T and NT-proBNP suggest mild myocardial strain, though the patient remains hemodynamically stable (PESI Class III - moderate risk).

Therapy initiated in ED:
- Oxygen 2L/min via nasal cannula
- Enoxaparin 80 mg (1 mg/kg) SC stat
- Normal Saline 500 ml IV bolus

Plan:
Admission to the Department of Pulmonology for further monitoring and initiation of oral anticoagulant therapy (Apixaban). Monitor for signs of hemodynamic instability.
Follow-up with vascular surgery for post-thrombotic syndrome prevention.
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate for the presentation.

report_content:
**Emergency Department Report (Synthetic)**

**Surname and Name:** ŠIMIĆ MARIN  
**Patient ID (MBOO):** 284915603  
**Address:** PUT DRAČEVCA 22, SPLIT  
**Date of Arrival:** 06.02.2026, 18:24  
**Date of Discharge:** 06.02.2026, 22:10  
**Date of Birth:** 11.09.1983 (42 years)  
**Sex:** Male  
**Triage Category:** 2 (very urgent)

---

## Diagnoses
- **T78.2 Anaphylactic shock, unspecified**  
- **L50.0 Allergic urticaria**

---

## Triage Data (18:28)
- **BP:** 86/54 mmHg  
- **Pulse:** 118 /min  
- **SpO₂:** 92% (room air)  
- **Resp:** 26 /min  
- **Temp:** 36.7 °C  
- **VAS:** 2/10 (throat tightness)  
- **GCS:** 15

**Reason for Arrival:** Generalized rash, lip swelling, throat tightness, dizziness after food ingestion.

---

## Anamnesis
**History of Present Illness:**  
Patient reports sudden onset of generalized itching and hives approximately **20–30 minutes** after eating at a restaurant (seafood platter; unsure of exact ingredients). Within minutes developed **lip swelling**, **tightness in throat**, hoarse voice, **shortness of breath**, and **dizziness** while standing. No loss of consciousness. No chest pain. No fever. EMS transported to ED; no medications administered pre-hospital per patient.

**Past Medical History:**  
- Seasonal allergic rhinitis  
- No known asthma  
- No known cardiovascular disease

**Medications:**  
- Cetirizine PRN (not taken today)

**Allergies:**  
- **No known drug allergies**  
- Food allergies not previously diagnosed

**Habits:**  
- Non-smoker  
- Alcohol occasionally

**Functions:**  
- Appetite normal until event  
- Urination/stool without complaints

---

## Physical Examination (18:35)
**General:** Anxious, oriented, speaking in short sentences, pale, diaphoretic, **eupneic but tachypneic**, hoarse voice.  
**Skin/Mucosa:** Diffuse urticaria on trunk and upper limbs; **perioral/lip edema**.  
**Head/Neck:** No visible tongue protrusion; **oropharynx mildly edematous**, no exudate.  
**Lungs:** Bilateral **expiratory wheeze**, prolonged expiration.  
**Heart:** Tachycardic, regular rhythm, no murmurs.  
**Abdomen:** Soft, non-tender, no guarding.  
**Extremities:** No edema; capillary refill ~3 sec; peripheral pulses present.

---

## Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
### ECG (18:40)
- Sinus tachycardia ~115/min  
- No ST elevation/depression, no acute ischemic changes

### Laboratory Results (19:05)
**CBC:**
- WBC **13.2 ×10⁹/L** (NEU 82% / 10.8 ×10⁹/L)
- RBC 4.89 ×10¹²/L  
- HGB 147 g/L  
- Hct 0.435 L/L  
- Platelets 262 ×10⁹/L

**Biochemistry:**
- Glucose 6.8 mmol/L  
- Urea 5.9 mmol/L  
- Creatinine 86 µmol/L (eGFR 97 mL/min/1.73m²)  
- AST 18 U/L, ALT 16 U/L  
- CRP 3.2 mg/L  

**Electrolytes:**
- Na 140 mmol/L  
- K 3.7 mmol/L  
- Cl 104 mmol/L  
- Ca 2.30 mmol/L  

**Venous blood gas (19:10):**
- pH 7.36  
- pCO₂ 44 mmHg  
- HCO₃⁻ 24 mmol/L  
- Lactate **2.6 mmol/L**

### Imaging
**Chest X-ray (PA portable, 20:05):**  
- No acute infiltrate or pleural effusion; cardiac silhouette normal; mild peribronchial markings.

---

## Therapy / ED Course
- IV access x2  
- **0.9% NaCl bolus 1000 mL IV**, then 250 mL/h  
- **Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV**  
- **Chloropyramine (Synopen) 20 mg IM**  
- **Salbutamol nebulization 2.5 mg** once (wheeze improved)  
- Oxygen via nasal cannula 2–3 L/min (SpO₂ improved to 96%)

**Reassessment:**
- 19:20 BP 94/61, HR 106, SpO₂ 96% on 2 L O₂; urticaria persistent but less pruritus; voice still mildly hoarse.  
- 21:30 BP 104/68, HR 92, SpO₂ 97% room air; minimal residual rash; lip edema decreased; no stridor.

---

## Conclusion / Disposition
Clinical picture consistent with **anaphylaxis** triggered by likely food allergen (seafood possible). Patient initially hypotensive with respiratory involvement (wheeze/hoarseness) and diffuse urticaria. After fluids, corticosteroid, antihistamine, bronchodilator, and observation, he improved hemodynamically and respiratorily without recurrence of symptoms over ~3.5 hours.

**Discharged home** with instructions:
- Avoid suspected triggers (seafood/restaurant meal components) until allergology evaluation.  
- Cetirizine 10 mg 1×1 for 3 days.  
- Prednisone 40 mg daily for 2 days (then stop).  
- Arrange allergology outpatient testing within 2–4 weeks.  
- Return immediately or call EMS for any recurrence of lip/tongue swelling, breathing difficulty, dizziness, or generalized rash.

Verbal and written instructions provided.

---
gold_reference: This presentation meets criteria for anaphylaxis (hypotension plus respiratory involvement and mucocutaneous findings). First-line, life-saving treatment is immediate intramuscular epinephrine. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjunctive and do not treat airway edema or shock. Omitting epinephrine risks deterioration, biphasic reaction, and death.

Extremities: No edema, calves soft and non-tender.

12-Channel ECG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 100/min. Normal axis. No ST-T wave changes suggesting ischemia.
Radiographic Imaging of Heart and Lungs (PA and Lateral)
Findings:
Right lower lobe consolidation consistent with lobar pneumonia. No pleural effusion. Cardiac silhouette is within normal limits. Hilar structures are unremarkable. Trachea is midline.

Hematology - Complete Blood Count:
WBC 16.5↗ (1e9/L); Erc 4.80 (1e12/L); Hb 142 g/L; Hct 0.420 L/L; MCV 88.0 fL; Plt 280 (1e9/L)

    • 500 mL 0.9% NaCl IV
Prescribed for Home:
    • Clarithromycin 500 mg 1 tablet every 12 hours orally for 7 days
    • Paracetamol 500 mg orally as needed for fever
Conclusion
Patient presents with clinical and radiological signs of right lower lobe pneumonia. Vital signs are stable following antipyretics; SpO2 remains >94% on room air. CURB-65 score is 1 (Age >65), suggesting low risk, suitable for outpatient management.

gold_reference: Clarithromycin is a strong CYP3A4 inhibitor. Simvastatin is metabolized by CYP3A4. Co-administration significantly increases simvastatin plasma concentrations, leading to a high risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.

Of medium musculoskeletal build and nourishment, conscious, communicative, oriented in time and space, mobile, afebrile, eupneic at rest, non-icteric.
